role_summary: Boston Celtics center 1956-1969; 11-time NBA champion; first Black head coach in NBA history (1966); civil rights advocate.
biographical_paragraph: Bill Russell was born February 12, 1934 in Monroe, Louisiana, and died July 31, 2022 in Mercer Island, Washington at age 88 [src: 1]. He played center for the Boston Celtics from 1956 to 1969, winning eleven NBA championships, and in 1966 became the first Black head coach in the NBA, winning two further championships as player-coach [src: 1]. He participated in the 1961 Celtics Lexington boycott: before the 1961-62 season his team was scheduled to play an exhibition game in Lexington, Kentucky, and after Russell and his Black teammates were refused service at a local restaurant, he and they refused to play and flew home [src: 1]. He attended the 1967 Cleveland Summit, a gathering of African-American athletes and political leaders convened to support Muhammad Ali's refusal to be drafted [src: 1]. His oral history is held by the Library of Congress as entry 8 of the Civil Rights History Project [src: 2].

sources.title: Bill Russell oral history
sources.publisher: Civil Rights History Project, Library of Congress
